answer:
**'Hāh' (ch. 11) and 'Nawā-e-Gham' (ch. 19) both appear in *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, Iqbal's 1924 Urdu poetry collection, confirming they share the same volume.[^3]**

Both poems operate in a lament-register, framed as cries ('Hāh' = "Alas!" or an interjection of grief; 'Nawā-e-Gham' = "The Melody of Sorrow"). Their rhetoric coincides in rejecting deferred consolations—afterlife paradises or unplucked potential—in favor of the immediate, vital *pulse* of existence: youth's burning longing in 'Hāh', the lute-like life's silent songs in 'Nawā-e-Gham'. Exclamations ("Ah, Youth!", "Alas!") and metaphors of contained fire/energy (youth's "hopeful longing burn", life's "bosom holds all songs") underscore a shared urgency to seize or elevate the present amid existential frustration.[^1][^2]

**'Hāh' stakes its territory in defiant carpe diem for youth**, scorning Houris, Saqi, and Paradise as "too late" for a soul craving instant joy:  
> How strange and wondrous is this very pulse of life!  
> For Youth's one creed is *'The Joy of Today,'* beyond all strife.[^4]

Its most charged line pulses with wonder at life's immediacy, prioritizing earthly "beauty" that "needs [no] practiced eye" over eschatological bait.[^5]

**'Nawā-e-Gham' carves out sorrow (*gham*) as soul-elevating ascent**, likening life to a "silent, waiting lute" whose "hopeless song" rises via divine sigh from Sinai, culminating in transformative height:  
> As dew is raised by its desire for flight,  
> My nature finds in sorrow's song its height.[^6]

Its peak charge lies here, where grief's "marching-bell" (*nawā*)—not joy—frees the "imprisoned" soul, nearest in corpus to *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*'s "Philosophy of Sorrow" (ch. 7).[^7]

'Hāh' claims youth's bold now; 'Nawā' sorrow's redemptive strain—rhetorically twin laments, tonally divergent paths.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 11 "Hah", [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/11-hah/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 19 "Nawa-e-Gham", [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/19-nawa-e-gham/translation/).

[^3]: `list_chapters` + wiki cards for *Bang-E-Dara Part 2* (works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/wiki/_work.card.md).

[^4]: [p-3e10ab](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/11-hah/translation/#p-3e10ab).

[^5]: TextRank key passage [p-52b7ac](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/11-hah/translation/#p-52b7ac) (via read_chapter).

[^6]: [p-a766ad](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/19-nawa-e-gham/translation/#p-a766ad); TextRank/LexRank #1-2.

[^7]: Wiki nearest: Part 3 ch. 7 cosine 0.16 (works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/wiki/19-nawa-e-gham.full.md).
